questions.question: How does the Health and Safety Executive (HSE) define work-related violence?
options: A written complaint made by a relative
Only a physical assault that leaves a visible injury
Any disagreement between two members of staff during a shift
Any incident in which a person is abused, threatened or assaulted in circumstances relating to their work

questions.question: What does the Betari Box help you understand?
options: A method for scoring how aggressive a person is
How your attitude and behaviour feed into the other person's attitude and behaviour in a loop
The safest way to physically restrain a person
The legal duty to report an incident within 24 hours

questions.question: After a person reaches the peak of anger and begins to calm down, why should you avoid rushing them back into a task?
options: Pushing too soon can trigger a second escalation
It is against the law to continue care after an outburst
The person will no longer remember the incident
Recovery always takes exactly one hour

questions.question: Which question best reflects a trauma-informed approach to a person's distress?
options: Why is this person being so difficult?
What happened to this person?
How can I make this person comply?
What is wrong with this person?

questions.question: Positive behaviour support focuses on changing things around the person to prevent distress, always using the:
options: least restrictive option
strongest medication available
same fixed routine for everyone
quickest form of physical control

questions.question: What does 'dynamic risk assessment' mean during a developing conflict?
options: Judging the risk moment by moment as the situation unfolds and adjusting what you do
Assessing the risk only after the incident is over
Leaving the risk decision entirely to your manager
Completing a written risk form once a year

questions.question: According to the material, physical intervention should be:
options: used routinely to keep people calm
carried out by any staff member who feels threatened
the first response whenever a person refuses care
a last resort, used only by staff trained in it and never as punishment

questions.question: Which statement best describes passive communication?
options: Standing up for your needs clearly while still respecting the other person
Giving in and not stating your needs, which can leave issues unresolved and build resentment
Shouting and using threats to get your own way
Calmly offering the person a choice

questions.question: You are lone working in the community. Which action follows good lone-working practice?
options: Keep your visits secret from colleagues to protect the person's privacy
Share your schedule, keep a charged phone and any alarm, and check in as agreed
Leave your phone in the car so it does not disturb the visit
Stay in a home that feels unsafe until the person calms down

questions.question: According to the course, conflict happens when:
options: someone has been physically injured
the needs, wishes or expectations of two or more people clash
a person deliberately sets out to cause trouble
a care worker makes a mistake during a task

questions.question: A relative is angry about a missed visit. The material suggests that what sounds like a complaint is frequently:
options: a sign the family should be avoided
an attempt to get a staff member sacked
always an unreasonable demand
worry about someone they love

questions.question: When resolving a disagreement with a colleague, the material advises you to focus on:
options: who is most to blame for the situation
avoiding the disagreement altogether
winning the argument in front of others
the issue and its effect, not the person's character

questions.question: Research on face-to-face contact quoted in the course suggests that, when calming someone, which often carries more meaning?
options: Your tone of voice and body language
The exact words you choose
The length of your explanation
How quickly you speak

questions.question: During de-escalation, why does the material say you should avoid correcting, blaming or trying to 'win'?
options: It wastes time that should be spent restraining the person
It breaks data protection rules
The person is always right
Arguing only fuels the conflict

questions.question: A trauma-informed approach asks 'what happened to this person?' rather than 'what is wrong with them?'.
options: True
False

questions.question: Physical restraint may be used routinely by any care worker to keep a person under control.
options: True
False

questions.question: Verbal abuse and threats count as work-related violence under the HSE definition, even when no physical assault takes place.
options: True
False

questions.question: Withdrawing from an unsafe situation to a safe distance and calling for help means you have failed as a carer.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are recognised warning signs that a person's aggression may be building? (Select all that apply)
options: Smiling warmly and stepping back to give you room
Clenched fists, pacing or a squared-up stance
A fixed stare and reddening of the face
Sudden silence, sarcasm or invading your space
A relaxed, open posture and calm breathing

questions.question: Which principles support resolving conflict with a colleague constructively? (Select all that apply)
options: Focus on the issue and its effect, not the person's character
Listen first so the other person feels heard before you explain your side
Try to win the argument at all costs
Find common ground, as you usually share the goal of good, safe care
Take sides in corridors and share the disagreement as gossip

questions.question: What does a trauma-informed, person-centred approach involve? (Select all that apply)
options: Using physical intervention as a routine first response
Focusing only on controlling the person's behaviour
Offering safety, choice and predictability so people do not feel cornered
Using the least restrictive option to prevent distress
Asking what happened to the person rather than what is wrong with them

questions.question: Which questions form part of a dynamic risk assessment as a situation unfolds? (Select all that apply)
options: Is this getting safer or more dangerous?
Who is to blame for starting this?
How can I win this argument?
Is anyone else at risk?
What is my way out?

questions.question: The ___ Box describes a loop in which your attitude shapes your behaviour, which affects the other person's attitude, which then shapes their behaviour back towards you.
Physical intervention should only ever be used as a last ___, by staff trained in it, and never as routine or punishment.
Judging the risk moment by moment as a situation unfolds, and adjusting what you do, is called ___ risk assessment.
